VolkswagenA Global Brand Analysis ‹ Final Project, ADV 853

The People's Car, a global century in motion.

A research project on the past, present, and advertising legacy of Volkswagen, one of the most studied global brands in marketing history. From a single factory in 1937 Berlin to a 150-country footprint, this site explores how a humble German car became a global cultural force.

Founded 1937, Berlin
Markets 150+ Countries
Largest Region Western Europe (42%)
Strategy NEW AUTO
PROJECT OVERVIEW

What you'll find on this site.

A deep dive into the past, present, and advertising legacy of Volkswagen. Explore the three core sections below.

The Author's Mk3 Golf, Philadelphia My '98 Volkswagen Golf GTI, the visceral, no-nonsense hatchback that started my love for the brand. While my friends bought BMW M4s and Audi S5s, this is what taught me the Volkswagen ethos.
PAGE 01

History & Global Growth.

The People's Car: A Vision for the Masses

The Volkswagen Type 1, the original "People's Car" that defined the brand's identity for over half a century.

Volkswagen's history began in 1937 in Berlin, Germany, under the name Gesellschaft zur Vorbereitung des Deutschen Volkswagens mbH. The company was established with the sole purpose of manufacturing a "People's Car", an affordable, quality vehicle that every German citizen could theoretically own. In its earliest years, the brand operated strictly in the automotive manufacturing sector, focusing on a single product originally referred to as the "Porsche Type 60," which would later become the world renowned Volkswagen Beetle. However, the business's early years were defined by hardship; the outbreak of World War II redirected the factory to the arms industry, where it produced military variants like the Kübelwagen using forced labor. Mass production of the civilian "People's Car" only truly began after the war, when British Major Ivan Hirst saved the plant from dismantling, recognizing that the beautifully simple, air-cooled design was exactly what a recovering global economy needed.

Car Culture and the Wörthersee Phenomenon

The Volkswagen Type 2 Bus, an icon of 1960s counterculture and one of VW's most globally exported designs.

Beyond pure manufacturing, Volkswagen has cultivated a unique global car culture rooted in community and modification. This is best exemplified by the Wörthersee Treffen in Austria, which began in 1982 as a small gathering of Golf GTI enthusiasts and exploded into the world's largest Volkswagen group festival. This phenomenon aligns with the Viral Branding Model, where a brand's desirability is driven by "brand tribes", enthusiastic communities that reinvent the brand's meaning through their own rituals and vocabulary. For decades, Wörthersee became the ultimate stage for showcasing your love for you Volkswagen and the brand.

WÖRTHERSEE TREFFEN
A glimpse of Wörthersee Treffen in Austria across multiple years. The 2017 entrance arch on the lake, the 2009 GTI Treffen with fans dipping their feet by the water, restored Volkswagen Things, modified Mk1 Golfs, lineups of fan-built cars, and even a stone monument carved in the shape of a Golf in the town of Reifnitz. The brand tribe at the heart of the Viral Branding Model.

Strategic Global Expansion: Acting Globally, Thinking Locally

Volkswagen's growth is a masterclass in the "Global-Local Paradox." VW didn't just sell cars, they built local identities. In the mid-1950s, VW made a strategic move into South America, specifically Brazil, after U.S. imports stopped due to a shortage of dollar reserves. By 1953, VW was assembling cars in Brazil, eventually creating models like the Brasilia that were designed specifically for the local market. In the 1980s, VW became a pioneer in China, establishing a "transnational" footprint that allowed them to dominate the market by adapting to local infrastructure.

Today VW exemplifies Stage 3 of Global Development: Local Products, Local Marketing Communications. As highlighted by the book, while the core engineering of a vehicle such as the Golf remains a global standard, VW's ability to act globally through a massive production alliance while thinking locally in its advertising efforts as well, allowed it to maintain its position as the powerhouse it is across 150 different countries.

PAGE 02

Current Brand Analysis.

Industry & Global Market Presence

The Volkswagen ID. Buzz, the centerpiece of VW's electric era and a deliberate visual nod to the iconic Type 2 Bus.

Volkswagen remains a titan in the global automotive industry, operating in over 150 countries with a production network that spans every major continent. As of early 2026, its largest market remains Western Europe, accounting for nearly 42% of total global deliveries. However, its strategy is heavily defined by its performance in China, which represents its second-largest territory, and South America, where the brand is currently seeing a strong growth surge (+7.0%) in a stabilizing economic environment. This diverse geographical spread is a practical application of the Global Marketing Mix, managing a unified global brand while adjusting sales strategies to fit the regional economic changes.

Strategic Framework: "NEW AUTO"

Volkswagen's current strategy is focused on moving from a traditional hardware manufacturer to a software-driven mobility company. This transformation is guided by a clear strategic framework:

Mission Statement

"To offer mobility for everyone."

Vision Statement

"By 2035, we aim to be 'The Global Automotive Tech Driver', shaping mobility for people, now and in the future"

Core Values

Customer focus, top performance, value creation, and a unique blend of Innovation with Tradition. This last value is crucial for fans of the brand's history, as it seeks to maintain the "soul" of the company while transitioning to the electric "ID." family of vehicles.

The 2019 VW logo refresh, flat, two-dimensional, and built for digital. Each principle of the new mark is shown around the central icon: clear and simple, flat, contrasting, focused, flexible, open, easy to use, and reduced to the essentials.
The Volkswagen Golf GTI lineup, the "hot hatch" that built the Wörthersee fan culture.

PAGE 03

Advertising Analysis.

Six iconic Volkswagen advertisements, analyzed through the lens of textbook concepts learned in class.

Ad #1, 1959, DDB
AD ANALYSIS, 01

Think Small

1959, Print

Creative Analysis

This is perhaps one of the most famous minimalist ads in history. It features a tiny Beetle in a vast white space with a simple two word tagline. The copy is humble, pointing that the car is not fast or big, but it's reliable.

Textbook Connection

This ad reflects a low power distance and low masculinity appeal. In a 1950s America that valued bigger is better, VW successfully appealed to a counter culture by emphasizing honesty and functional value over ego.

Reaction

I chose this because it is the "DNA" of the brand's simplicity. It proves that you don't need flash to be effective.

Ad #2, 1960s
AD ANALYSIS, 02

Keeping Up with the Kremplers

1960s, Print

Creative Analysis

A neighbor (Mr. Jones) spends $3,000 on one big flashy car. Meanwhile, the Kremplers spend $3,000 and get a refrigerator, a range, a washer, a dryer, two TVs, and a Volkswagen.

Textbook Connection

This is a direct attack on Conspicuous Consumption. It appeals to short term vs. long term orientation, showing that the sensible choice allows for a higher quality of life overall rather than just status in the driveway.

Reaction

This perfectly captures the "People's Car" philosophy. It's about utility and the smart use of resources, which is why the brand has remained relevant for nearly a century at this point.

Ad #3, 1990, TV
AD ANALYSIS, 03

The Squeaky Earring

1990, Television

Creative Analysis

A man is driven to madness by a tiny squeaking sound in his car, only to realize at the end that the "noise" was coming from his wife's loose earring instead of from the car.

Textbook Connection

This ad targets High Uncertainty Avoidance cultures (like Germany and Italy). It promises that the car is so perfectly built that even the slightest imperfection must be external, appealing to the consumer's need for precision and reliability.

Reaction

It's a brilliant way to show "quality" without showing a factory. It uses a relatable human moment to prove a technical point.

Ad #4, 2006, TV
AD ANALYSIS, 04

Un-pimp Your Auto

2006, TV Campaign

Creative Analysis

Featuring a German engineer (Peter Stormare) literalizing the "destruction" of over the top modified cars to replace them with a factory-tuned GTI. It uses humor, high-energy "crashes," and the catchphrase "V-Dub: Representing Deutschland".

Textbook Connection

This is a classic Individualistic appeal. It targets the "creator" who wants to stand out but values "German Engineering" (Uncertainty Avoidance) over superficial modifications.

Reaction

As a child, seeing this ad shaped my view of the Volkswagen brand. Creating a very memorable watching experience and being shown that the experience of a Volkswagen comes from the engineering instead of the aesthetics on the outside.

Ad #5, 2010, TV
AD ANALYSIS, 05

Old Lady

2010, Television

Creative Analysis

A father and son are inspecting a used Golf being sold by a sweet old lady. They assume the car was driven gently because of the sellers appearance, However, a montage reveals she drove it like a rally car. The ad ends by saying you can't depend on every old lady, but you can depend on every Golf.

Textbook Connection

This ad plays with Stereotypes vs. Reality. It appeals to the "hidden" performance of the Golf, suggesting that beneath the "People's Car" exterior is a machine capable of extreme durability.

Reaction

It highlights the "visceral" side of the Golf that I love. It's a "sleeper" car, it looks simple, but it has a soul that likes to be pushed.

Ad #6, 2019, TV
AD ANALYSIS, 06

Hello Light

2019, TV (post-Dieselgate)

Creative Analysis

This ad marks the pivot from the 2015 emissions crisis to the electric future. It shows a designer in a dark room working on the new ID. Buzz, accompanied by Simon & Garfunkel's "The Sound of Silence."

Textbook Connection

This is a Recovery Strategy focusing on Sustainability and Long-Term Orientation. After the scandal (a blow to the brand's "honesty"), VW used this ad to reset its "Strategic Framework," moving toward a vision of clean, "light" mobility.

Reaction

I find this ad incredibly powerful because it doesn't run away from the past; it uses the "darkness" of the scandal to literally "turn on the lights" for the electric era.

REFERENCES

  • Volkswagen Group, The Group Strategy
